title: Real AI Threats vs. Media Hype

modules.title: Why Hype and Underreaction Are Both Security Risks
modules.sub: Understanding how distorted threat perception undermines security decisions
modules.description: Learners examine real organizational case studies where both panic-driven overreaction and dismissive underreaction led to measurable security failures. They map the cognitive and institutional pressures that pull security teams toward distortion, and commit to a standard of calibrated, evidence-based threat assessment.

modules.title: A Framework for Evaluating AI Threat Claims Critically
modules.sub: Building a repeatable methodology for scoring AI threat credibility
modules.description: Learners are introduced to a structured evaluation framework that scores AI threat claims across dimensions such as evidence quality, technical feasibility, and observed prevalence. They practice applying the framework to sample threat claims and produce a scored credibility rating with supporting rationale.

modules.title: High-Probability AI Threats Already Affecting Organizations Today
modules.sub: Identifying AI-enabled attacks with documented, real-world organizational impact
modules.description: Learners analyze confirmed, evidence-backed AI threats — including spear phishing at scale, automated vulnerability discovery, and deepfake-assisted fraud — using current incident data and threat intelligence. They assess the realistic likelihood and impact of each threat category and identify which deserve immediate defensive prioritization.

modules.title: Overhyped AI Threats: What the Evidence Actually Shows
modules.sub: Stress-testing high-profile AI threat narratives against available evidence
modules.description: Learners take widely circulated AI threat scenarios and run them through the evaluation framework to expose gaps between media framing and documented reality. They practice writing concise, evidence-anchored rebuttals to inflated claims that they can use when briefing leadership or budget committees.

modules.title: How Vendors and Media Inflate AI Threat Narratives
modules.sub: Recognizing the incentives and tactics that manufacture AI threat noise
modules.description: Learners decode the commercial and editorial incentives that lead vendors, analysts, and journalists to amplify AI threat narratives beyond what evidence supports. They identify specific rhetorical patterns — vague capability claims, worst-case framing, missing base rates — so they can flag them automatically when consuming new threat content.

modules.title: Translating Threat Assessment into Proportionate Defensive Investment
modules.sub: Converting calibrated threat scores into defensible resource allocation decisions
modules.description: Learners use their scored threat assessments to build a prioritized defensive investment map, matching resource allocation to evidence-based risk levels rather than headline urgency. They draft a proportionate recommendation brief designed to communicate trade-offs clearly to non-technical stakeholders and executives.

modules.title: Running a Threat Reality Check Inside Your Organization
modules.sub: Operationalizing the evaluation framework as an ongoing organizational practice
modules.description: Learners design a lightweight, repeatable threat reality check process tailored to their organization's size, sector, and existing security workflows. They produce a practical rollout plan — including roles, cadence, and decision triggers — so the framework becomes a durable team habit rather than a one-time exercise.

synopsis: Security teams that chase AI doomsday headlines waste budget and attention while real AI-enabled threats — spear phishing at scale, automated vulnerability discovery, deepfake fraud — quietly succeed. This course builds a rigorous, evidence-based methodology for separating credible AI threats from noise so defenders can invest protective effort where it actually matters.
rationale: The gap between AI threat hype and operational reality is causing both dangerous complacency about genuine risks and costly overinvestment in speculative ones, with no widely taught framework for telling the difference.
learning_outcome: Learners can apply a structured threat-evaluation framework to any AI security claim, produce a calibrated risk assessment, and present proportionate defensive recommendations to stakeholders.
source_signals: Ask HN: Is AI threat overblown?
AI cybersecurity is not proof of work
